medications.drug_name: empirical oral antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate) — per in-person severity
medications.indication_rationale: For the soft-tissue infection; choice/route (oral vs IV) and duration decided at in-person High-Risk Foot/ED assessment based on severity and cultures.
medications.dose_route_frequency: e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily (as per source note; per prescriber)
contraindications_in_this_case: Penicillin allergy — use alternative; escalate to IV if severe/osteomyelitis

medications.drug_name: strict offloading (non-weight-bearing / total-contact device) and no barefoot walking
medications.indication_rationale: Removing pressure is essential for healing; a post-op stiff-soled shoe/walker boot and strict offloading are as important as antibiotics.

medications.drug_name: simple home dressing as definitive management
medications.indication_rationale: A plaster/home dressing alone with watchful waiting is inadequate and dangerous for a limb-threatening infected diabetic foot ulcer.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: No barefoot walking; wear protective enclosed footwear even indoors, and the daughter should inspect the foot twice daily.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents further silent trauma and detects deterioration early.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take diabetes medications/insulin as prescribed and work urgently on glycaemic control.

patient_education_points.point: Because the nerves in your foot are damaged, you can't feel this wound — but that does NOT mean it's minor. A deep infected diabetic foot wound can threaten your leg and needs to be seen in person today.
Keep all weight off the foot and never walk barefoot. Take the antibiotics, and your daughter should check the foot twice a day.
If the redness spreads up the foot or leg, the foot turns black, or you develop a fever, chills or confusion — call an ambulance (000).

follow_up_plan.interval: Same-day High-Risk Foot Service / ED; GP within 24-48h to coordinate
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Wound depth/probe-to-bone, imaging for osteomyelitis, infection response, offloading device, glycaemic control.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Ascending redness, blackening of the foot, fever/chills/confusion — call 000.

must_include_items: Recognise a limb-threatening infected neuropathic diabetic foot ulcer despite painlessness
Arrange urgent same-day in-person High-Risk Foot Service/ED assessment (probe-to-bone, imaging for osteomyelitis)
Empirical antibiotics AND strict offloading / no barefoot walking
acceptable_alternatives: ED vs direct High-Risk Foot Service depending on availability/severity
IV antibiotics if systemically unwell or osteomyelitis suspected
errors_of_omission: Not recognising the limb threat / reassured by painlessness
No urgent in-person referral or osteomyelitis evaluation
errors_of_commission: Advising a simple home dressing/plaster with watchful waiting
Antibiotics alone without offloading or referral
Reassuring because there is no pain or fever
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: LIMB-THREAT CASE: failing to escalate an infected neuropathic diabetic foot ulcer for urgent in-person assessment (being reassured by painlessness), or omitting offloading, is a critical failure.
